narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one, with about a 73% chance of not passing. Picture your story through its lens: it started with your Year 1 grades landing you in the 2nd decile at school, a middling start that signaled early struggles in keeping up academically, and that became the second-biggest red flag pulling toward failure. By Year 3, things hadn't shifted much—you were in the 3rd decile, and this was the model's top concern, whispering that your performance hadn't built the steady momentum top bar passers usually show. Even with your high family income offering a safety net others might lack, the model saw it as oddly tilting against you, perhaps linked to patterns where that backdrop doesn't always translate to grit under pressure. Your LSAT score of 35 was solid but not a standout rescue, ranking fourth in pushing the prediction downward, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 added another layer of quiet concern about foundational readiness. Being a white male in a full-time program didn't buck the trends the model knows all too well, with those traits lining up fifth through eighth in reinforcing the risks. It's this mosaic—decent pieces that together form a portrait of someone who's capable but whose academic deciles paint a picture of consistent bottom-third positioning—that led to the tough call on your application. You're not defined by numbers, though; this is just one snapshot, and with targeted steps like bolstering study habits or retaking key tests, you could rewrite that story. Hang in there—many have turned similar predictions around. What do you think your next move will be?
